attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with acute urinary retention and lower abdominal pain. He underwent a transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) five days ago and was discharged yesterday. He has a medical history of benign prostatic hyperplasia and hypertension. His regular medications include duodart-tamsulosin and amlodipine.

On examination, he appears uncomfortable. His pulse rate is 90/min, blood pressure 145/90 mmHg, respiratory rate 26/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. His lower abdomen is distended and tender to palpation. A 16 Fr urethral catheter is inserted, but only a small amount of blood-stained urine drains.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with signs and symptoms consistent with urinary clot retention following a recent TURP. The inability to drain urine through the catheter and unsuccessful irrigation attempts suggest the presence of clots obstructing the catheter or filling the bladder. In this scenario, the most appropriate next step is manual bladder washout.

Manual bladder washout involves using a large-bore catheter (typically 22-24 Fr) and manually irrigating the bladder with normal saline using a catheter-tip syringe. This procedure aims to break up and evacuate clots, restoring bladder drainage. It is the first-line treatment when standard catheter irrigation fails and should be attempted before more invasive procedures.

Intravenous antibiotics may be necessary if there are signs of infection, but they are not the primary treatment for clot retention. In this case, there is no clear evidence of infection, and addressing the obstruction is the priority.

Suprapubic catheter insertion is an alternative method of bladder drainage but is more invasive and typically reserved for cases where urethral catheterisation is not possible or has failed. In this scenario, a urethral catheter is already in place, and the issue is clot obstruction rather than inability to catheterise.

Cystoscopy and clot evacuation is a more invasive procedure that may be necessary if manual bladder washout fails to clear the clots. However, it is not the first-line approach and should be considered only if less invasive methods are unsuccessful.

Computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis may be useful in some cases to evaluate for complications or to rule out other causes of symptoms. However, in this case, the clinical picture is clear, and imaging would delay definitive treatment of the clot retention.

After successful manual bladder washout, continuous bladder irrigation should be initiated to prevent recurrence of clot formation. The patient should also be monitored for ongoing bleeding and potential need for surgical intervention if bleeding persists or recurs.
learningPoints: Urinary clot retention is a urological emergency that can occur after prostate or bladder surgery, or in patients with bladder tumours.
Manual bladder washout is the first-line treatment for urinary clot retention when catheter irrigation fails.
references: NHS Bristol Urological Institute. Bladder Washouts Standard Operating Procedure (SOP). [online]. Available at: https://www.nbt.nhs.uk/sites/default/files/Bladder%20washout%20SOP.pdf

Olapade-Olaopa EO, Solomon LZ, Carter CJ, Ahiaku EK, Chiverton SG. Haematuria and clot retention after transurethral resection of the prostate: a pilot study. Br J Urol. 1998 Nov;82(5):624-7. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/9839574/

Kusljic S, Aneja J, Manias E. Incidence of complications in men undergoing transurethral resection of the prostate. Collegian. 2017;24(1):3-9. Available at: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1322769615000542
